rulings.Case Title: THOMAS SEGAL VS. FELIX K. CHING et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Pltf'S Attendance And Testimony At Depo
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, May 27 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANT FELIX CHING's Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Attendance And Testimony At Deposition Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The motion has been assigned to Judge Pro Tem Bruce Highman. He has a workers' compensation insurance policy with State Farm. There are no claims pending against this policy. That he has this workers' comp policy does not affect his decision on this motion in any way. The parties may address any concerns with this disclosure at the hearing. The unopposed motion is granted. Sanctions of $520 are awarded against plaintiff. The parties shall be prepared to discuss the date of the deposition and the date by which sanctions should be paid. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN KOOPMAN VS. RED AND WHITE FLEET et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: ON CALENDAR FOR TUESDAY, MAY 27, 2014, LINE 5, DEFENDANT RAMOS OIL COMPANY, INC DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT The demurrer is overruled. The existence of a duty is usually determined by a summary judgment motion because the analysis is somewhat factual. For instance, it is a factual issue whether there have been prior similar incidents. At any rate, plaintiff pleads sufficient facts to create a duty under Rowland v. Christian, (1968) 69 Cal.2d 108, 113. The injury was generally foreseeable, defendant owns the hoses, and imposing a duty to warn or instruct on defendant is not burdensome. Plaintiff correctly notes that the doctrine of assumption of the risk does not apply to admiralty cases. See De Sole v. United States (1991 4th Cir.) 947 F.2d 1169, 1174. Lastly, the worker's compensation argument is untimely because it was raised in the reply brief and it is also frivolous. Worker's compensation exclusivity does not apply because plaintiff and defendant do not have an employment relationship. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/EHG

rulings.Case Title: GABRIELA GEYER et al VS. MERIDIAN MANAGEMENT GROUP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs' Motion For Sanctions For Failure To Obey A Court Order Against Meridian Management Group And Its Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, May 27 2014, Line 3, Plaintiffs' Motion For Sanctions For Failure To Obey A Court Order Against Meridian Management Group And Its Counsel Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted in part and denied in part. Monetary sanctions of $7,500 are awarded against both Meridian Management and its attorneys and shall be paid by June 17. Meridian Management and its counsel should have known and informed plaintiffs before any work on discovery motions was done that they did not have certain requested information as to 305 Hyde Street so that time of both plaintiff's attorneys and the court was not wasted and so that plaintiff could have immediately sought the information from the third party. Because defendant didn't do this and instead just contested the motion, and then negotiated the terms of the order, the court issued an order to produce documents as to 305 Hyde Street with which defendant Meridian Management has not complied. The failure to comply is therefore the fault of Meridian Management and its counsel. Issue and evidentiary sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highman-ball.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: EVAN C NELSON VS. TUCKER ELLIS, LLP
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Special Motion To Strike
rulings.Rulings: ON CALENDAR FOR TUESDAY, MAY 27, 2014, LINE 11, Defendant'S Special Motion To Strike Granted. The Court must engage in a two-step process when ruling on an Anti-SLAPP motion under CCP sec. 425.16. "First, the court decides whether the defendant has made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity¿If the court finds such a showing has been made¿it then determines whether the plaintiff has demonstrated a probability of prevailing on the claim." (Equilon Enterprises v. Consumer Cause Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 53, 67.) Defendant made a threshold showing that the cause of action is one arising from protected activity. In determining whether the defendant has satisfied their burden under the first prong of section 425.16 analysis, the focus is whether "the defendant's activity that gives rise to his or her asserted liability" is in furtherance of a person's right of petition or free speech. (Feldman v. 1100 Park Lane Associates (2008) 160 Cal.App.4th 1467, 1478.) The activity giving rise to Defendant's asserted liability is producing documents in response to a subpoena. (Complaint, pars. 8-9, 38-39.) Responding to a subpoena is a protected activity. (Greka Integrated, Inc. v. Lowry (2005) 133 Cal.App.4th 1572, 1580.) Further, even though the Complaint alleges that the documents produced were not within the scope of the subpoena, "communications made in connection with litigation do not necessarily fall outside the privilege merely because they are, or alleged to be, fraudulent, perjurious, unethical, or even illegal, assuming they are logically related to the litigation." (Blanchard v. DirectTV, Inc. (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 903, 921.) Plaintiff has not demonstrated a probability of prevailing on any causes of action. The litigation privilege (Civ. Code sec. 47(b)) bars all of the causes of action because all of the causes of action arise from Defendant providing documents in response to a subpoena. (See Foothill Federal Credit Union v. Sup. Ct. (2007) 155 Cal.App.4th 632.) Defendant's request for attorney's fees is denied without prejudice. Defendant may file a separate fee motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/EHG

rulings.Case Title: EXPERT COMMUNICATIONS, INC VS. GAIL TANAKA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Expert Communications, Inc.'S Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: ON CALENDAR FOR TUESDAY, MAY 27, 2014, LINE 15, Plaintiff Expert Communications, Inc.'S Order To Show Cause Re Preliminary Injunction Granted. (CCP § 527.) Plaintiff has demonstrated a likelihood of prevailing on the merits on the breach of contract claim. Defendant has not demonstrated that she will suffer substantial harm. In regards to Defendant's privacy concerns, the preliminary injunction shall include a protective order that: (1) limits the contents of the three drives to use for purposes of this action; and (2) limits access to the contents of these drives to the third party forensic expert only. The forensic expert may identify the names of files, documents, fragments or deleted files/documents to both parties and their counsel. Defendant waived any bond requirement in execution of the Confidentiality Agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
